review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for diagnostic questions about temporal shift invariance and the advantage of sliding local temporal windows in 3D convolution.

question_bank.question: Why does early-fusion 2D convolution lack temporal shift invariance?
choices: Its filters extend across the full time dimension, so the same motion at different times may require different filters
It only works on grayscale videos
It pools over time before any convolution happens
Its filters are local in time and slide across frames
question_bank.explanation: If a filter spans the entire temporal axis at once, it is tied to where a motion pattern appears in time. A repeated pattern occurring earlier or later may not be matched by the same filter.

question_bank.question: What advantage does a 3D convolution get from using a local temporal window that slides over time?
choices: It can reuse the same filter to detect a motion pattern at different time positions
It guarantees zero computational overhead compared with 2D CNNs
It removes the need for any pooling layers
It prevents the network from learning spatial features
question_bank.explanation: A local temporal window makes motion detection more like ordinary spatial convolution. The same kernel can respond to a pattern wherever it appears in time, not just at one fixed position.
